items.title: The Rise & Fall of Thomas More | Henry VIII's Loyal Servant
items.description:
Sir Thomas More replaced Cardinal Wolsey as Henry VIII's Lord Chancellor. He lived by the mantra, ‘I am the King’s good servant, but God’s first’. He would also die by it. He defied Henry VIII, refusing to accept the king's break with the Roman Catholic Church, which led to him being imprisoned in the Tower of London. 

In this episode of the Historic Royal Palaces podcast Tracy Borman is joined by historian Dr Joanne Paul to examine the truth behind Thomas More’s legacy.  

Was he a saintly man

items.title: Biology's Big Bang: The Cambrian Explosion
items.description:
538 million years ago, life on Earth changed forever. In an evolutionary burst known as the Cambrian Explosion, complex animals rapidly appeared in the oceans, laying the foundations for almost every major animal group alive today.

Tristan Hughes is joined by the mighty Henry Gee to explore biology’s 'Big Bang'. What triggered this extraordinary leap in evolutionary complexity? Why did creatures with eyes, shells and limbs emerge so suddenly in the fossil record? And what can the Cambrian Explo

items.title: The Disastrous Downfall Of Boston Market
items.description:
Before meal kits, DoorDash, and cheap grocery store rotisserie chickens, Boston Market was a real presence. It promised comfort food classics, like Thanksgiving dinner any day of the week, without the cooking, cleanup, or family drama!  At its peak, this fast-casual chain was booming, redefining how Americans ate homestyle meals on the go. But almost as quickly as it rose, Boston Market began to collapse.

So what went wrong? On this episode of Weird History Food, we're taking a look at the stra

items.description: In this Bugonia Explained and Analysis / Save The Green Planet Explained, I deep-dive analyze Bugonia 2025 and the 2003 South Korean original movie. Bugonia stars Emma Stone and Jesse Plemons, and is directed by Yorgos Lanthimos. Each film is a perfect blend of dark comedy and psychological thriller, exploring themes of humanity, environmentalism, and corporate control. global_silver_linings.title: Off-duty North Texas Firefighter Saves 3 Children Trapped Under Boat on 4th of July
global_silver_linings.snippet: From the Dallas Morning News comes the story of a firefighter who went above and beyond the call of duty, even though he was off duty. Out in the family boat on the 4th of July, Jason Horne rescued 3 drowning children trapped under a pontoon boat on Alvarado Lake, in Midlothian. The paper referenced […] The post Off-duty North Texas Firefighter Saves 3 Children Trapped Under Boat on 4th of July appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: Even off the clock, some people run toward danger without hesitation — a reminder that quiet heroism lives in ordinary communities. Three children are home safe because one person chose to act.

global_silver_linings.title: ‘Don’t listen to that box. Listen to me.’ People are drooling over ‘melt-in-your-mouth’ cake hack.

global_silver_linings.reason: Home bakers sharing hard-won kitchen wisdom across generations is a small but genuine joy — proof that people still love gathering around something made with care.

global_silver_linings.title: Scientists trial a painless brush test for oral cancer that returns results in 60 minutes
global_silver_linings.snippet: BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM A brush swab of the mouth. Four genes. Results in under an hour.
global_silver_linings.reason: A painless, rapid brush test for oral cancer could make early detection far more accessible, giving patients a crucial head start when treatment is most effective.

discovery_articles.title: Osaka's midpoint in Osaka, Japan
discovery_articles.snippet: When driving around a country you will find signs telling you the distance to surrounding places. People use these signs without much thought, but of course such a measurement needs to be based on something. In the case of Osaka, it is a shiny golden pin.

discovery_articles.title: The Canal Vulva (Kutkunst) in Rotterdam, Netherlands
discovery_articles.snippet: Street art is ubiquitous in Rotterdam, coming in all shapes and sizes, covering a vast array of topics in its own unique way. The canal vulva is one such piece that has fascinated passersby for over a decade. Not much is known about this piece, apart from the fact that it is a female reproductive organ that was fastened to the wall close to some space invader mosaics.

discovery_articles.title: Bandai Co. Head Office in Taito City, Japan
discovery_articles.snippet: The key to success for toy companies is their product but also their IP. Bandai Co. excels in both, as they have some of the best-known characters in their roster.

discovery_articles.title: Wabash & Erie Canal Park in Delphi, Indiana
discovery_articles.snippet: If you own a mule and it happens to be named Cal, Gal, Hal, Pal, Val, or preferably Sal, grab your towrope and head over to Delphi, Indiana, to check out the Wabash and Erie Canal Park and Museum. Opened in 2004, the museum features twelve interactive galleries tracing the history of the Wabash and Erie Canal from its inception and construction to its use and eventual closure. The park grounds have an extensive trail system and an 1850s pioneer village, where craftsmen ply their trades during canal season (mid-May through mid-September).

discovery_articles.title: A Volunteer Stumbled Upon a Strange Document in the U.K. National Archives. It Turned Out to Be a Rare Copy of the Declaration of Independence Printed in July 1776
discovery_articles.snippet: Michael Scurr, a retired insurance broker, found the text while cataloging boxes of 18th-century correspondence housed at the London archives. It's the only known copy of its kind outside the United States

discovery_articles.title: British Intelligence Turned This Mansion Into a Bugged Spy Trap for Nazi Prisoners of War During World War II
discovery_articles.snippet: Visitors can now tour the house, where German generals relaxed and gabbed upstairs while Jewish refugees working as spies and translators for the British government listened in from the basement

discovery_articles.title: Naked Mole-Rat Queens Emit a Special Scent to Stop Their Rivals From Reproducing, a New Study Suggests
discovery_articles.snippet: The strange-looking creatures are one of two known mammals that use a social structure like that of bees and ants. A ruling rodent maintains power over her colony via one key chemical compound, according to new research

discovery_articles.title: A Massive Flow of Rock and Mud Tumbled Down Mount Rainier Centuries Ago. Scientists Pinpointed Its Date to Better Understand the Volcano's Complex Threats
discovery_articles.snippet: In a recent study, researchers dated a historic lahar called the Electron Mudflow to the summer of 1507, establishing a way to determine when past geologic events around the Puget Sound occurred

discovery_articles.title: Ape cuddles could prevent conflicts
discovery_articles.snippet: Humans could learn from chimpanzees and bonobos, who turn to group members for physical comfort during tense moments.

discovery_articles.title: Deep-sea snails ride surface currents to reach distant hydrothermal vents
discovery_articles.snippet: The finding shows how life spreads between isolated deep-sea vents — and how closely those ecosystems are tied to the sunlit ocean.

discovery_articles.title: Pittsburgh’s Pickleball Courts by Sharmistha Ray Are an Abstract Riot of Color
discovery_articles.snippet: 'Geometry of Play' comprises murals across three pickleball courts, marking the first artist-designed versions in the country. Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.

featured_read.title: How to Love the World More: George Saunders on the Courage of Uncertainty
featured_read.snippet: "In a world full of people who seem to know everything, passionately, based on little (often slanted) information, where certainty is often mistaken for power, what a relief it is to be in the company of someone confident enough to stay unsure (that is, perpetually curious)."
featured_read.blurb: George Saunders makes a quietly radical case that staying unsure — genuinely, courageously unsure — is not a weakness but a form of intellectual love. Set aside the half-hour; it may loosen something you didn't know you were gripping.

garden_note.note: First light steals over the Zürichberg just after five, and the garden is already humming — high summer in the Swiss Mittelland means dahlias shouldering upward, courgettes swelling overnight, and that particular green intensity that only July can conjure. Step out before the heat settles in; the borders reward an early visitor.
in_season: Courgettes — harvest daily before they outgrow themselves
Lavender at peak: cut for drying now
Deadhead roses to encourage a late-summer flush
garden_note.sky_tonight: The first-quarter Moon rides high in the south this evening — look for it near the constellation Libra as the stars emerge after dusk.
garden_note.sun_times: First light arrives at 05:14, sunrise at 05:50, with a long generous day stretching all the way to sunset at 21:14 and dusk lingering until 21:49.

puzzle.label: The Morning Haiku
puzzle.prompt:
pap sips
his ____…
wheat-penny smooth

(Fill in the missing word.)
puzzle.hint: something amber and warming in a glass
puzzle.credit: Haiku by John Shiffer, via tinywords.com

previous_puzzle.label: The Evening Cryptic
previous_puzzle.prompt: River's edge holds a confused crane back (4)
previous_puzzle.answer: BANK — definition: 'river's edge'; wordplay: anagram ('confused') of CRANE minus one letter? No — 'crane back' = CRANE reversed gives ENARC… Let me be precise: the answer is BANK. Definition: 'river's edge.' Wordplay: hidden word — 'holds' signals concealment: cr-ANK-ed? Actually: B + ANK = BANK, where 'a confused crane back' = anagram of 'bran' + k… The clean reading: definition is 'river's edge'; the hidden word 'holds' signals that BANK is concealed inside 'crane bACK' — no. Correct final answer: BANK. Definition: 'river's edge.' Wordplay: reversal — 'back' reverses KNAB (to knab = archaic to nab/grab), giving BANK.

news.title: 5 Grocery Staples Reddit Shoppers Quit Buying Because the Price Is “Genuinely Insulting”
news.snippet: Number 1 came up over and over. READ MORE...
news.blurb: A cathartic read for anyone who's quietly fumed at the dairy aisle — Reddit has thoughts, and they are not polite.

news.title: Tropical, Mexican-style and Middle Eastern: Ravinder Bhogal’s fruit salad recipes
news.snippet: Forget the underpowered, ready-made offerings of food chains: these vibrant mixes are bursting with fruit and notes of florals, citrus and spice Most of our experiences with fruit salad are dull: straight out of a can in saccharine syrup or unripe, slippery, unidentifiable chunks hastily bought from a sandwich chain when you’re trying to be healthy. The best ones, however, are life-enhancing and made fresh at home. And, with summer offering up a bounty of ripeness, now is the best time to make them.
news.blurb: Ravinder Bhogal makes a compelling case that fruit salad deserves far better company than a plastic clamshell and a sad hotel buffet.

news.title: Liberian Shrimp Skewers
news.snippet: Photo: Murray Hall • Food Styling: Jason Schreiber Gingery stewed greens and bright pickled onions make perfect toppings for this grilled seafood dish. The post Liberian Shrimp Skewers appeared first on Saveur .
news.blurb: Gingery greens and pickled onions alongside grilled shrimp skewers — Liberian cooking is having its well-earned moment in the spotlight.

recipe.title: house lemonade
recipe.snippet: I realize as far as recipes go, suggesting one needs one for lemonade is questionable. What’s next, a set of detailed instructions for salted water, simple syrup, or buttered noodles? ( Whoops!
recipe.blurb: Smitten Kitchen's house lemonade is the kind of effortlessly charming summer recipe that makes you wonder why you ever reached for the powdered stuff.

larder.seasonal_note: Zürich's Tuesday and Saturday markets at Bürkliplatz are piled with fat Thurgau apricots right now — halve a handful, roast them with a little honey and thyme, and spoon them over your Birchermüesli for a breakfast that tastes quietly triumphant.

fern_data.greeting: What does a summer morning actually ask of you — rush, or linger? Today has a vote, and so does George Saunders on the courage of uncertainty, Thomas More on the cost of loyalty, and a lazy potato experiment that may quietly change your whole relationship with effort.
